perturbation.original_text: Proprietary Information shall be marked as proprietary, confidential or with another suitable legend at the time of disclosure or otherwise designated in writing as proprietary by the Disclosing Party within thirty (30) days of disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Proprietary Information shall be marked as proprietary, confidential or with another suitable legend at the time of disclosure or otherwise designated in writing as proprietary by the Disclosing Party within ninety (90) days of disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the designation timeframe to 90 days contradicts the general understanding and legal interpretation that trade secrets must be actively protected to maintain their status. A 90-day delay in designation could be seen as a failure to take reasonable measures to protect the information, potentially leading to the loss of trade secret status under Pennsylvania law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under the Pennsylvania Uniform Trade Secrets Act (PUTSA), a key element of maintaining trade secret status is demonstrating reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. Delaying the formal designation of information as proprietary for 90 days could be interpreted as a failure to take such reasonable measures, potentially jeopardizing its legal protection as a trade secret, especially if the information is disclosed during that period.

Cons. Stat. 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated & Unconsolidated Statutes Title 12 - COMMERCE AND TRADE Chapter 53 - Trade Secrets Section 5302 - Definitions Universal Citation: 12 PA Cons Stat § 5302 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 5302. Definitions. The following words and phrases when used in this chapter shall have the meanings given to them in this section unless the context clearly indicates otherwise: "Improper means." Includes, but is not limited to, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy or espionage through electronic or other means. "Misappropriation." Includes: (1) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (ii) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was: (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. "Person." A natural person, corporation,
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the timeframe for designating proprietary information from 30 to 90 days. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both suggest this delay could be interpreted as a failure to take reasonable measures to maintain secrecy, potentially jeopardizing trade secret status under Pennsylvania law. Snippet 2 defines 'Misappropriation' and implies a duty to maintain secrecy, which the 90-day delay may undermine. While Snippet 2 doesn't explicitly state a timeframe for designating proprietary information, the emphasis on maintaining secrecy supports the argument that a longer delay could weaken trade secret protection, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement without cause by giving thirty (30) days prior written notice of termination to the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement without cause by giving ten (10) days prior written notice of termination to the other party.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period to 10 days may be deemed unreasonably short, especially if it affects trade secrets that are in use.
perturbation.law_explanation: While contract law allows for termination clauses, courts may scrutinize clauses providing for extremely short notice periods, particularly if such termination would unfairly prejudice one party. What constitutes 'reasonable notice' depends on the context and the nature of the agreement. Given that this is a Non-Disclosure Agreement with potentially ongoing obligations related to confidential information, ten days' notice may be deemed insufficient to allow the receiving party to adequately protect the disclosing party's interests, potentially leading to a breach of the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing which Pennsylvania recognizes.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: contract | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you contract A contract is an agreement between parties , creating mutual obligations that are enforceable by law . The basic elements required for the agreement to be a legally enforceable contract are: mutual assent , expressed by a valid offer and acceptance ; adequate consideration ; capacity ; and legality . In some states , elements of consideration can be satisfied by a valid substitute. Possible remedies for breach of contract include general damages , consequential damages , reliance damages , and specific performance . Background : Contracts are promises that the law will enforce. Contract law is generally governed by state common law , and while general overall contract law is common throughout the country, some specific court interpretations of a particular element of the contract may vary between the states. If a promise is breached, the law provides remedies to the harmed party, often in the form of monetary damages, or in limited circumstances, in the form of specific performance of the promise made. Elements -- Consideration and Mutual Assent Contracts arise when a duty comes into existence, because of a promise made by one of the parties. To be legally binding as a contract, a promise must be exchanged for adequate consideration. There are two different theories or definitions of consideration: Bargain Theory of Consideration and Benefit-Detriment theory of consideration. Benefit-Detriment Under the b enefit-detriment theory, an adequate consideration exists only when a promise made to the benefit of the promisor or to the detriment of the promisee, which reasonably and fairly induces the promisor to make a promise for something else for the promisee. For example, promises that are purely gifts are not considered enforceable because the personal satisfaction the grantor of the promise may receive from the act of generosity is normally not considered sufficient detriment to constitute adequate consideration. Bargain-for-Exchange Under Bargain-for-Exchange theory of consideration, adequate consideration exists when a promisor makes a promise in return for something else. Here, the essential condition is that the promisor was given something specifically to induce the promise being made. In other words, the bargain for exchange theory is different from the detriment-benefit theory in that the focus in bargain for exchange theory seems to be the parties’ motive for making the promises and the parties’ subjective mutual assent, while in detriment benefit theory, the focus seems to be an objective legal detriment or benefit to the parties. Governing Laws Contracts are mainly governed by state statutory and common (judge-made) law and private law (i.e. the private agreement). Private law principally includes the terms of the agreement between the parties who are exchanging promises. This private law may override many of the rules otherwise established by state law. Statutory law, such as the Statute of Fraud , may require some kinds of contracts be put in writing and executed with particular formalities, for the contract to be enforceable. Otherwise, the parties may enter into a binding agreement without signing a formal written document. For example, Virginia Supreme Court has held in Lucy v. Zehmer that even an agreement made on a piece of napkin can be considered a valid contract, if the parties were both sane, and showed mutual assent and consideration. Most of the principles of the common law of contracts are outlined in the Restatement of Law, Second Contracts published by the American Law Institute. The Uniform Commercial Code , whose original articles have been adopted in nearly every state, represents a body of statutory law that governs important categories of contracts. The main articles that deal with the law of contracts are Article 1 (General Provisions) and Article 2 (Sales) . Sections of Article 9 (

perturbation.justification: The original text provided for thirty (30) days prior written notice, whereas the changed text reduces this period to ten (10) days. This creates a potential legal contradiction because the Law Explanation suggests that courts may scrutinize termination clauses with extremely short notice periods, especially in cases involving confidential information where a shorter notice might prejudice one party and potentially violate the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. Although neither scraped law snippet directly addresses the length of a termination notice, the LLM Law Explanation indicates that a 10-day notice could be deemed insufficient, particularly considering ongoing obligations related to confidential information. The "Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction" definition also states that a legal contradiction happens when a contract sets a deadline or requirement that violates federal or state law, making the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal, which applies here.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be effective as of the Effective Date below and shall expire three (3) years after the Effective Date.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be effective as of the Effective Date below and shall expire one (1) year after the Effective Date.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the agreement's lifespan to one year creates a potential conflict because the confidentiality obligations extend for five years.
perturbation.law_explanation: Though this NDA extends the confidentiality of the information for 5 years, the overall agreement is only for 1 year. This creates a contradiction of terms within the agreement. There is no legal requirement for the exact length of time that an NDA must be in effect, this could be interpreted as the agreement being voided due to conflicting terms.
perturbation.justification: The original agreement had a lifespan of three years, while the changed version reduces it to one year. The Perturbation Explanation notes this creates a potential conflict because the confidentiality obligations extend for five years. The Law Explanation further elaborates that this discrepancy could void the agreement due to conflicting terms. The absence of relevant scraped law snippets doesn't negate the contradiction created by the conflicting terms within the agreement itself, where a shorter agreement lifespan clashes with a longer confidentiality period. Since the confidentiality obligations outlast the agreement's lifespan, this creates an internal inconsistency, satisfying the definition of 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: The obligation of confidentiality and non -disclosure with respect to Proprietary Information shall expire on the later of the date: (i) that is five (5) years from the Effective Date below; or (ii) when the Proprietary Information is no longer protected as a trade secret under applicable law.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligation of confidentiality and non -disclosure with respect to Proprietary Information shall expire on the later of the date: (i) that is one (1) year from the Effective Date below; or (ii) when the Proprietary Information is no longer protected as a trade secret under applicable law.
perturbation.explanation: Shortening the confidentiality period to one year could be problematic under the PUTSA if it is not a 'reasonable time' to retain the trade secret's value.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Pennsylvania Uniform Trade Secrets Act (PUTSA) does not specify an exact length for how long a trade secret should be protected, but it requires the information to be subject to efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain secrecy. Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be seen as not reasonable and therefore would jeopardize the company's ability to assert trade secret protection under PUTSA. This is especially the case if the information remains valuable and is kept secret beyond that one-year period.
perturbation.justification: The original contract provides a confidentiality period of five years or until the information is no longer a trade secret. The modified contract reduces this confidentiality period to one year, or until the information is no longer a trade secret. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both indicate that reducing the confidentiality period to one year may not be 'reasonable' under the PUTSA, potentially jeopardizing trade secret protection, because the information might remain valuable and secret beyond the one-year period. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition, where time-sensitive obligations (the confidentiality period) do not align with legal requirements (reasonableness under trade secret law), making the contractual term potentially unenforceable.
